topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with an irritating intensity, mirroring the churning in my gut. I watched Marcus meticulously pack his gear, his movements too smooth, too practiced. Every placement of his shin guards, every snap of the zipper, felt like a deliberate slight against the entire group. "Alright, Marcus," Coach Miller began, his voice overly cheerful, a tone that grated on my nerves like sandpaper. "We've made some decisions about the roster for the upcoming tournament." My jaw tightened, a muscle twitching near my ear. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush that had nothing to do with exertion. When the inevitable came—the word—I kept my expression neutral, a mask of forced calm, but inside, a cold, hard knot formed in my chest. I just wanted the whole thing to end so I could walk out into the cold air and never have to see his smug face again.

[story 2]

I stood by the water cooler, pretending to examine the condensation on the plastic bottle, but my eyes kept darting toward the bench where Liam sat, radiating an irritating sort of self-satisfaction. It was nauseating. Every time he laughed at one of my muttered comments—comments I didn't even intend to make, just small irritations bubbling up—a sharp, unpleasant sensation shot through me. My hands clenched into fists inside my pockets, the knuckles turning white. When the coach finally called my name, the sound felt distant, muffled. I forced myself to turn, meeting his gaze with a blankness that felt brittle. "Marcus," I managed, the sound coming out rougher than I intended. I didn't look at him, focusing instead on a scuff mark on the floor. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, and I could feel the pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate need to just push him away, to make him disappear from my sight.

[story 3]

The air in the gym was thick with stale sweat and unspoken tension. I kept my focus on the dribbling drill, trying to channel the rising tide of resentment into pure, focused energy. But it was impossible. Every time I saw David effortlessly execute a move I struggled with, a bitter taste coated my tongue. It was a constant reminder of what I wasn't, what I lacked. Coach Miller approached me, his shadow falling over me, and the casualness of his tone made my skin crawl. "Listen up, everyone. We have to make some tough choices regarding the team structure." My breath hitched. I felt a sudden, intense urge to physically recoil, to shrink into myself. When he named David, my entire body seemed to lock up. I didn't speak. I just stared at the polished floor, my gaze fixed on a tiny imperfection, feeling a deep, internal churning—a profound sense of unfairness that settled heavy and immovable in my core.
